design_philosophy.core_loop: Build and optimize transport networks to generate profit; manage vehicles, routes, and infrastructure across multiple transport modes (rail, road, sea, air) over a century-long campaign
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Competition against AI rivals and economic self-sustainability; balancing infrastructure investment against cash flow and loan repayment while managing vehicle lifecycle and route efficiency
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Operating a transport empire tycoon; starting with borrowed capital and building a dominant transportation company through smart route planning and resource management
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasizes emergent long-term strategic planning over arcade action; imposes hard economic constraints (loans with interest, maintenance costs, vehicle depreciation) that force players to think systematically; no artificial win conditions beyond profit maximization; allows pausing/saving at any time to reduce pressure and encourage experimentation; technology progression is time-locked rather than achievement-gated, creating natural difficulty scaling

difficulty.continues_system: Game can be continued indefinitely past the 2030 end date, though score tracking stops; player can reload any saved game at any time
difficulty.checkpointing: Full save system available at any point during gameplay; multiple saves per game permitted; real-time gameplay can be paused for decision-making
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Extensive: difficulty options include competitor AI speed/aggressiveness, terrain generation variety, town/industry density, subsidy multipliers, disaster frequency, and economic cycle type (steady vs cyclical). Players can also customize game length (century spans ~20 minutes per in-game year), currencies, and map parameters before starting. Single-player focused with optional multiplayer via serial/modem

progression.upgrade_system: Technology tree locked to game years: steam engines available early (1930s), diesel and electric engines introduced mid-game, monorails appear in 1999. Vehicles improve through manufacturer prototype offerings with better stats but initial reliability issues. Player-driven expansion: can upgrade existing routes or expand network as capital allows. Station rating system affects cargo attraction—higher ratings through efficient transfers improve revenue
progression.unlock_mechanism: Time-gated technology; no achievement-based unlocks. New vehicle models are automatically introduced by manufacturers in appropriate years and gradually replace older models. Player must construct appropriate depots (rail yards, bus stations, airports, shipyards) to access each transport type
replayability_hooks: Randomly generated maps for each new game
Adjustable difficulty settings alter economic pressure and competition
Multiple transport mode combinations allow different strategic approaches
AI opponents with variable skill provide different competitive dynamics
Open-ended profit-maximization incentivizes varied route networks and optimization strategies

world_building_techniques: Procedurally generated towns and industries create living economic ecosystems
Time progression (1930-2030) mirrors real-world transportation history
Town councils create dynamic relationships—player reputation affects construction permissions
Disasters and vehicle crashes introduce emergent narrative events
Commodity flow between industries and towns creates implicit supply-chain storytelling
Rival AI companies serve as ambient antagonists competing for market share

faction_indicators: AI rival companies with distinct identities (player names and colors)
Town council relationships reflecting player actions and reputation
Transport network dominance as visible power projection across the map
danger_signals: Bankruptcy risk if loans cannot be serviced—economic failure state
Competitor expansion threatening market control of key routes
Disasters (crashes, malfunctions) causing financial and operational disruption
environmental_storytelling: Visible transport networks map player company's economic reach and priorities
Vehicle variety and modernization reflect strategic choices and tech adoption
Industry-town connections show economic specialization and trade patterns
Route density in regions indicates wealth concentration and market competition
